steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction / choledocholithiasis, biliary stricture, or periampullary pathology causing CBD dilation; marginal ulcer or anastomotic complication post-gastric bypass
ensemble.information_gap: The OSH CT showed a dilated CBD without an identified cause (no stone, no intrahepatic ductal dilation), and the gallbladder itself was not adequately characterized; the physician needs to evaluate the gallbladder (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, stones, sludge) and reassess the biliary tree with a modality optimized for biliary anatomy.
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstones or biliary sludge (common after gastric bypass), gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid suggesting cholecystitis, or further characterization of the CBD caliber and any periductal abnormality that could explain the dilated CBD seen on OSH CT.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The OSH CT identified a dilated CBD without a clear etiology and did not characterize the gallbladder; abdominal ultrasound is the first-line, radiation-free modality specifically optimized for evaluating the biliary tree and gallbladder, and is directly responsive to this unresolved imaging finding in a post-gastric-bypass patient with elevated lipase and epigastric pain.
grounding: History: 'A CT done at the OSH showed a dilated CBD without evidence of gallstones, CBD stone, or intra-hepatic ductal dilatation'
History: 's/p gastric bypass surgery ___ years ago'
Laboratory tests: 'Lipase: 172.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — approximately 3x upper limit of normal
Laboratory tests: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.8 mg/dL', 'AST: 15.0 IU/L', 'ALT: 13.0 IU/L', 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 59.0 IU/L' — liver function tests normal despite dilated CBD
History: '3 days of epigastric pain... burning... nausea... vomiting'
Physical examination: 'Abd: soft, NT, ND, +BS' — no peritoneal signs
ensemble.reasoning: This post-gastric-bypass patient has a dilated CBD on OSH CT without an identified obstructing lesion, a lipase nearly three times the upper limit of normal, and epigastric pain with nausea/vomiting — a constellation that raises concern for biliary pathology (choledocholithiasis, biliary sludge, or stricture) as well as pancreatitis, both of which are more common after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. The liver enzymes and bilirubin are paradoxically normal, leaving the etiology of CBD dilation unexplained. A dedicated right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the logical next step to directly visualize the gallbladder for stones or sludge, assess the CBD caliber at this institution, and look for cholecystitis — information the CT did not provide and that will guide whether ERCP, MRCP, or surgical consultation is needed.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction / choledocholithiasis, biliary stricture, or periampullary pathology causing CBD dilation; marginal ulcer or anastomotic complication post-gastric bypass
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The OSH CT showed a dilated CBD without an identified cause (no stone, no intrahepatic ductal dilation), and the gallbladder itself was not adequately characterized; the physician needs to evaluate the gallbladder (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, stones, sludge) and reassess the biliary tree with a modality optimized for biliary anatomy.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Gallstones or biliary sludge (common after gastric bypass), gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid suggesting cholecystitis, or further characterization of the CBD caliber and any periductal abnormality that could explain the dilated CBD seen on OSH CT.
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The OSH CT identified a dilated CBD without a clear etiology and did not characterize the gallbladder; abdominal ultrasound is the first-line, radiation-free modality specifically optimized for evaluating the biliary tree and gallbladder, and is directly responsive to this unresolved imaging finding in a post-gastric-bypass patient with elevated lipase and epigastric pain.

verification.actual_finding: The gallbladder was normal with no stones, sludge, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid; the CBD was markedly dilated at 1.5 cm with rapid distal tapering and slight intrahepatic ductal prominence, but no ductal stones were identified.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The expected gallbladder pathology (stones, sludge, cholecystitis) was absent, and while the ultrasound confirmed and further characterized the dilated CBD, it did not identify a causative lesion to explain the dilation.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction / choledocholithiasis; biliary stricture; periampullary or pancreatic head mass; post-gastric-bypass biliary anatomy abnormality
ensemble.information_gap: The cause of the markedly dilated CBD (1.5 cm with rapid distal tapering) remains unexplained — CT and ultrasound have excluded visible stones and intrahepatic ductal dilatation, but the nature of the distal obstruction or tapering point (stone, stricture, mass, or functional) is uncharacterized, and standard ERCP access is surgically altered by prior Roux-en-Y gastric bypass
ensemble.expected_finding: A filling defect consistent with a CBD stone, a focal stricture, or a periampullary/pancreatic head lesion at the point of rapid distal tapering that explains the ductal dilatation without visible stones on prior imaging
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive next step to characterize the distal CBD anatomy and identify the etiology of a markedly dilated CBD (1.5 cm) with rapid distal tapering when CT and ultrasound have been non-diagnostic for stones, and when the patient's Roux-en-Y gastric bypass anatomy makes standard ERCP technically challenging.
grounding: Prior imaging 1 FINDINGS: 'The CBD is markedly dilated, measuring up to 1.5 cm in diameter, with rapid distal tapering. No ductal stones are appreciated.'
History: 'A CT done at the OSH showed a dilated CBD without evidence of gallstones, CBD stone, or intra-hepatic ductal dilatation.'
History: 's/p gastric bypass surgery ___ years ago'
Labs: 'Lipase: 172.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — elevated ~3x upper limit of normal
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.8 mg/dL', 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 59.0 IU/L', 'AST: 15.0 IU/L', 'ALT: 13.0 IU/L' — liver function tests normal despite dilated CBD
Physical examination: 'Abd: soft, NT, ND, +BS' — no peritoneal signs
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a markedly dilated CBD (1.5 cm) with rapid distal tapering on both CT and ultrasound, yet no stone is visualized and liver enzymes are essentially normal — a pattern that raises concern for a distal obstructing lesion (stone too small to see, stricture, or mass) rather than simple choledocholithiasis. The mildly elevated lipase suggests possible biliary-pancreatic outflow involvement. Because her Roux-en-Y gastric bypass anatomy makes conventional ERCP difficult, MRCP is the logical next step to non-invasively characterize the distal biliary tree and identify the nature and level of obstruction before committing to an interventional approach.

verification.actual_finding: The CBD is dilated (up to 1.2 cm) with smooth distal tapering at the ampulla, no discrete obstructing mass, no stones, no abnormal enhancement, and the sphincter of Oddi was observed to open during the study, leaving the cause of dilatation unexplained.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected a filling defect, focal stricture, or periampullary/pancreatic head lesion to explain the distal tapering, but MRCP/MRI identified none of these — the obstruction point remains unexplained, directly contradicting the expectation of a characterizable structural cause.
